attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a four-hour history of right upper quadrant pain. The pain is constant in nature and is now beginning to settle. He feels nauseated but has not vomited. He has never had pain like this before. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He drinks four glasses of wine on weekends but does not smoke.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. His abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant but no guarding or rebound tenderness.

A CT scan of the abdomen is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely radiological diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The CT scan shows a reveals an incidental 3 cm multiloculated cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas with a central stellate calcification pattern (Image). This is the characteristic appearance of a pancreatic serous cystadenoma with its typical honeycomb pattern and central stellate calcification. Pancreatic cystadenomas are benign cystic neoplasms that occur more commonly in women over 60 years. While usually asymptomatic and found incidentally, they require follow-up due to a small risk of malignant transformation.

Gastric outlet obstruction would show a dilated stomach with fluid level and narrowing at the pylorus or duodenum. These findings are not present in the provided image.

Gallstones typically appear as high-density lesions within the gallbladder or bile ducts. While this patient's symptoms are suggestive of biliary colic, the CT finding in question is clearly within the pancreatic parenchyma.

Renal cell carcinoma typically appears as a solid, enhancing mass arising from the kidney, often with areas of necrosis or hemorrhage. The multiloculated cystic appearance in the pancreas is not consistent with this diagnosis.

Abdominal aortic aneurysm would appear as a dilated aorta with a diameter greater than 3 cm. The lesion in question is clearly within the pancreatic parenchyma and has characteristic features of a cystadenoma.

The management of pancreatic cystadenomas depends on size, symptoms, and patient factors. Small (<4 cm), asymptomatic lesions in elderly patients can often be monitored with serial imaging. Larger lesions or those causing symptoms may require surgical resection.
learningPoints: Pancreatic cystadenomas are typically benign cystic neoplasms that require follow-up due to malignant potential.
Incidental findings are common on abdominal CT scans and require appropriate risk stratification.
Serous cystadenomas have a honeycomb appearance on CT with central stellate calcification.
references: Behbahani S, Mittal S, Patlas MN, et al. ‘‘Incidentalomas’’ on abdominal and pelvic CT in emergency radiology: literature review and current management recommendations. Abdo Radiol (NY). 2017; 42:1046-1061
